one_line_takeaway: PriMod4AI is a RAG-based LLM pipeline that maps AI-system data flows to both classical LINDDUN privacy categories and a curated AI-specific privacy-attack taxonomy (membership inference, model inversion, extraction, etc.), showing PILLAR-comparable LINDDUN coverage (77.5-85.2% recall) but only moderate cross-LLM agreement (kappa 0.69-0.75) on the combined threat space where no ground truth exists to validate correctness.
key_finding: Layer A (Table III): for Face Authentication, PILLAR's own average category coverage was 71.4%; PriMod4AI(GPT-OSS) reached 85.0% PILLAR-Recall, 82.1% average coverage, 0.642 average Jaccard; PriMod4AI(LLaMA 3.1) reached 77.5% recall, 73.2% coverage, 0.617 Jaccard. For Autonomous Driving, PILLAR's coverage was 82.6%; PriMod4AI(GPT-OSS) reached 85.2% recall, 81.1% coverage, 0.726 Jaccard; PriMod4AI(LLaMA) reached 80.2% recall, 71.3% coverage, 0.686 Jaccard -- GPT-OSS consistently aligned more closely with PILLAR than LLaMA 3.1 on both systems. Layer B (Table III): cross-model (GPT-OSS vs. LLaMA3.1) agreement on the combined LINDDUN+AI-specific threat space was kappa=0.7455, Po=0.7818, PABAK=0.5782 for Face Authentication, and kappa=0.69, Po=0.715, PABAK=0.43 for Autonomous Driving; the paper's normalized robustness coefficient was R=0.7018 (Face Authentication) and R=0.6117 (Autonomous Driving), described as 'moderate and consistent' but 'not exceptionally high' cross-model reliability. The paper interprets these as showing PriMod4AI stays compatible with classical LINDDUN reasoning (via the PILLAR comparison) while additionally surfacing AI-specific, model-centric threats that PILLAR's LINDDUN-only design cannot represent.
core_contribution: PriMod4AI, a lifecycle-aware privacy threat-modeling framework for AI systems that (1) builds two structured knowledge bases -- a JSON-encoded LINDDUN taxonomy KB and a manually-curated AI-specific 'model-centric privacy attack' KB (membership inference, model inversion, training-data extraction, attribute inference, shadow-model reconstruction, embedding-space leakage) built from ~30 filtered peer-reviewed/regulatory sources spanning 2016-2025; (2) converts a system's Data Flow Diagram (DFD) into per-data-flow JSON metadata (source, destination, data_type, sensitive_info, lifecycle_stage); (3) embeds both KBs with MiniLM-L6-v2 and indexes them in FAISS, retrieving the top-k=7 nearest knowledge fragments per data flow via RAG; (4) assembles a fixed base prompt template (given in full in Appendix A) combining DF metadata + retrieved context + task instructions and feeds it to two open-source LLMs served via Ollama (GPT-OSS 120B and LLaMA 3.1 405B) in schema-constrained JSON mode to produce structured threat records (name, justification, linddun_category, ai_lifecycle_stage, source).
threat_model: The paper is a privacy-threat-modeling (methodology/tooling) paper, not an attack paper -- it does not implement or execute any attack itself. It reasons about two threat classes at the level of a system's data flows: (a) the seven classical LINDDUN privacy threat categories (Linkability, Identifiability, Non-repudiation, Detectability, Disclosure of information, Unawareness, Non-compliance), applicable to any data-processing pipeline; and (b) AI/ML-model-centric privacy attacks layered on top -- membership inference, model inversion, training-data extraction, attribute inference, shadow-model reconstruction, and embedding-space leakage -- attributed to models' memorization/overfitting behavior. The 'defender' is a privacy engineer analyzing a system's DFD pre- or post-design; the framework's job is to enumerate plausible threats per data flow and lifecycle stage (data collection, model building, training, deployment, inference, continuous monitoring), not to demonstrate exploitability.
limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) there is no expert-validated ground truth for the AI-driven/model-centric threat space, so Layer B could only measure inter-model agreement, not correctness or hallucination-freedom directly; (2) PILLAR itself is used only as a comparison point, not a ground-truth baseline, and PILLAR's outputs required human-analyst filtering before comparison, introducing a manual/subjective step into the baseline; (3) the AI Privacy KB was built via a single research team's structured-but-manual literature review (~30 sources after filtering) and is static, not continuously updated, so emerging attack types or regulatory changes are not automatically captured; (4) the Autonomous Driving system's real architecture was abstracted/generalized for confidentiality reasons, so results on that system reflect a simplified proxy rather than the original industrial system; (5) evaluation covers only two systems and two open LLM backends, decoding parameters were held fixed rather than tuned, and no mitigation guidance is generated for identified threats (left to future work). The paper also notes cross-model agreement was only 'moderate' (kappa in the 0.69-0.75 range), not near-perfect, meaning a meaningful fraction of identified threats are backend-specific rather than robustly reproduced.

datasets_used: Face Authentication System DFD (DF1-DF7), adapted from the open-source PILLAR GitHub repository
Autonomous Driving System DFD (DF1-DF14), derived and abstracted from the 'just better DATA' (JBData) government-funded research project
AI Privacy KB built from ~30 filtered peer-reviewed and regulatory sources (2016-2025) via structured literature review
LINDDUN KB converted from the official LINDDUN taxonomy PDF documentation into JSON
quantitative_results: Layer A Face Authentication: PILLAR avg. coverage 71.4%; PriMod4AI(GPT-OSS) 85.0% PILLAR-Recall / 82.1% avg. coverage / 0.642 avg. Jaccard; PriMod4AI(LLaMA 3.1) 77.5% recall / 73.2% coverage / 0.617 Jaccard
Layer A Autonomous Driving: PILLAR avg. coverage 82.6%; PriMod4AI(GPT-OSS) 85.2% recall / 81.1% coverage / 0.726 Jaccard; PriMod4AI(LLaMA 3.1) 80.2% recall / 71.3% coverage / 0.686 Jaccard
Layer B Face Authentication cross-model (GPT-OSS vs LLaMA3.1): Cohen's kappa=0.7455, Po=0.7818, PABAK=0.5782
Layer B Autonomous Driving cross-model: kappa=0.69, Po=0.715, PABAK=0.43
Robustness coefficient R=0.7018 (Face Authentication), R=0.6117 (Autonomous Driving)
RAG retrieval uses top-k=7 nearest knowledge fragments per data flow (MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings, FAISS index)
Semantic threat-name clustering merge threshold tau=0.20 (token-set Jaccard)
baselines_compared: PILLAR (LLM-based automated LINDDUN privacy threat modeling tool, run via its official Streamlit app with GPT-4 Turbo, temperature 0.7, output filtered by a human analyst) -- used as a comparison point, explicitly not treated as ground truth
PriMod4AI backend GPT-OSS (120B, via Ollama) vs. PriMod4AI backend LLaMA 3.1 (405B, via Ollama) compared against each other for cross-model agreement in Layer B
